text: ACCESS-400: Accessibility and Universal Design ● ACCESS-401: The platform must comply with WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) 2.1 Level AA standards. ● ACCESS-402: The entire interface must be fully navigable and operable using only a keyboard (e.g., Tab, Enter, Space keys). ● ACCESS-403: The interface must be compatible with screen reader software (e.g., NVDA, JAWS), including "alt" text for all images and "labels" for all form controls.

● ACCESS-402: The entire interface must be fully navigable and operable using only a keyboard (e.g., Tab, Enter, Space keys). ● ACCESS-403: The interface must be compatible with screen reader software (e.g., NVDA, JAWS), including "alt" text for all images and "labels" for all form controls. ● ACCESS-404: The UI must support browser-level text zoom up to 200% without breaking the layout or requiring horizontal scrolling. ● ACCESS-405: A "High-Contrast Mode" theme option must be available for users

● ACCESS-403: The interface must be compatible with screen reader software (e.g., NVDA, JAWS), including "alt" text for all images and "labels" for all form controls. ● ACCESS-404: The UI must support browser-level text zoom up to 200% without breaking the layout or requiring horizontal scrolling. ● ACCESS-405: A "High-Contrast Mode" theme option must be available for users with low vision. ACCESS-410: Multilingual Support ● ACCESS-411: The interface must support at least English and the native languages of the industrial partners. ● ACCESS-412: The user must be able to change the platform language from a "Settings" menu, and this preference must be saved. ● ACCESS-413: The UI design must be flexible enough to accommodate varying

ACCESS-410: Multilingual Support ● ACCESS-411: The interface must support at least English and the native languages of the industrial partners. ● ACCESS-412: The user must be able to change the platform language from a "Settings" menu, and this preference must be saved. ● ACCESS-413: The UI design must be flexible enough to accommodate varying text lengths from different languages (e.g., long words in German) without breaking the layout (i.e., no text overflow).

● ACCESS-411: The interface must support at least English and the native languages of the industrial partners. ● ACCESS-412: The user must be able to change the platform language from a "Settings" menu, and this preference must be saved. ● ACCESS-413: The UI design must be flexible enough to accommodate varying text lengths from different languages (e.g., long words in German) without breaking the layout (i.e., no text overflow). ACCESS-420: Training Integration ● ACCESS-421: A "Help" icon (?) must be present in complex sections (e.g., CDT simulator settings) that links directly to the relevant training module (T1.3) or video tutorial. ● ACCESS-422: All error messages must use language consistent with the T1.3

ACCESS-420: Training Integration ● ACCESS-421: A "Help" icon (?) must be present in complex sections (e.g., CDT simulator settings) that links directly to the relevant training module (T1.3) or video tutorial. ● ACCESS-422: All error messages must use language consistent with the T1.3 training materials and provide a clear suggestion for resolution (e.g., "Invalid date. Please use DD.MM.YYYY format."). ● ACCESS-423: A dedicated "Resources" or "Help Center" page must be available

FUNC-200: Information Presentation (Scoreboard) ● FUNC-201: The main dashboard must feature “timely scoreboard” components displaying critical data in a summarised manner, ensuring an adequate amount of information is shown to avoid overloading the view. ● FUNC-202: Each scoreboard element must provide direct access to its definition and interpretation, explaining what the metric represents and how it should be understood. ● FUNC-203: Scoreboard data must auto-refresh (near real-time) based on the

● FUNC-201: The main dashboard must feature “timely scoreboard” components displaying critical data in a summarised manner, ensuring an adequate amount of information is shown to avoid overloading the view. ● FUNC-202: Each scoreboard element must provide direct access to its definition and interpretation, explaining what the metric represents and how it should be understood. ● FUNC-203: Scoreboard data must auto-refresh (near real-time) based on the backend data stream, with a maximum latency of 1 minute.

● FUNC-202: Each scoreboard element must provide direct access to its definition and interpretation, explaining what the metric represents and how it should be understood. ● FUNC-203: Scoreboard data must auto-refresh (near real-time) based on the backend data stream, with a maximum latency of 1 minute. ● FUNC-204: Users must have an optional manual refresh action (e.g., refresh button) to request an immediate update of scoreboard data. ● FUNC-205: The user must be able to pause or manually trigger the auto-refresh

● FUNC-203: Scoreboard data must auto-refresh (near real-time) based on the backend data stream, with a maximum latency of 1 minute. ● FUNC-204: Users must have an optional manual refresh action (e.g., refresh button) to request an immediate update of scoreboard data. ● FUNC-205: The user must be able to pause or manually trigger the auto-refresh function for scoreboards. ● FUNC-206: Scoreboard elements must link to relevant training or help materials (e.g., tooltips, documentation pages, or tutorials) when available.

FUNC-210: Decision Support System (MO-DDSS) Interaction ● FUNC-211: The interface must present the user a list of optimization goals (e.g., energy efficiency, production efficiency, resource consumption, costs) and a dashboard through which users can set the priority (weights) of each objective for their own profile.

FUNC-210: Decision Support System (MO-DDSS) Interaction ● FUNC-211: The interface must present the user a list of optimization goals (e.g., energy efficiency, production efficiency, resource consumption, costs) and a dashboard through which users can set the priority (weights) of each objective for their own profile. ● FUNC-212: The optimization goals and their default priorities must be user-specific, reflecting the user’s role/team and configuration. ● FUNC-213: The interface must support a combination of manager-defined

● FUNC-211: The interface must present the user a list of optimization goals (e.g., energy efficiency, production efficiency, resource consumption, costs) and a dashboard through which users can set the priority (weights) of each objective for their own profile. ● FUNC-212: The optimization goals and their default priorities must be user-specific, reflecting the user’s role/team and configuration. ● FUNC-213: The interface must support a combination of manager-defined priorities (baseline/default weights) and a personalized section where users can

● FUNC-217: In “See Details” tab for each action there will be disclosed: 1) Track-explanation of the suggested actions; 2) Impact foresaw in the KPIs; 3) Step-by-step guidance on how to execute the action. ● FUNC-218: The interface will allow the user to select multiple simulation scenarios, such as the real/current production scenario or disruptive scenarios simulated with the C-DT. ● FUNC-219: Orders and tasks scheduling assessment. For a selected process/scope, the interface must present one or more recommended job

● FUNC-218: The interface will allow the user to select multiple simulation scenarios, such as the real/current production scenario or disruptive scenarios simulated with the C-DT. ● FUNC-219: Orders and tasks scheduling assessment. For a selected process/scope, the interface must present one or more recommended job scheduling scenarios and the forecasted impact on relevant KPIs. Users must be able to approve/apply a recommended scenario or adjust it and re-evaluate the impact before applying.

● FUNC-219: Orders and tasks scheduling assessment. For a selected process/scope, the interface must present one or more recommended job scheduling scenarios and the forecasted impact on relevant KPIs. Users must be able to approve/apply a recommended scenario or adjust it and re-evaluate the impact before applying. FUNC-220: System Monitoring and Alerts ● FUNC-221: The UI header must feature a persistent status icon indicating overall system health (e.g., Green/Online, Yellow/Warning, Red/Critical).

FUNC-220: System Monitoring and Alerts ● FUNC-221: The UI header must feature a persistent status icon indicating overall system health (e.g., Green/Online, Yellow/Warning, Red/Critical). ● FUNC-222: A non-intrusive toast notification (i.e., a small, temporary pop-up message that appears in a corner of the screen without blocking the user’s current task and auto-dismisses after a short time, optionally with a dismiss/action button) must appear when a critical alert is triggered. ● FUNC-223: An "Alert Center" page must exist, allowing users to view, filter (by

● FUNC-221: The UI header must feature a persistent status icon indicating overall system health (e.g., Green/Online, Yellow/Warning, Red/Critical). ● FUNC-222: A non-intrusive toast notification (i.e., a small, temporary pop-up message that appears in a corner of the screen without blocking the user’s current task and auto-dismisses after a short time, optionally with a dismiss/action button) must appear when a critical alert is triggered. ● FUNC-223: An "Alert Center" page must exist, allowing users to view, filter (by severity, date, module), and "acknowledge" all active and historical alerts.

● FUNC-222: A non-intrusive toast notification (i.e., a small, temporary pop-up message that appears in a corner of the screen without blocking the user’s current task and auto-dismisses after a short time, optionally with a dismiss/action button) must appear when a critical alert is triggered. ● FUNC-223: An "Alert Center" page must exist, allowing users to view, filter (by severity, date, module), and "acknowledge" all active and historical alerts. ● FUNC-223: An "Alert Center" page must exist, allowing users to view, filter (by severity, date, module), and "acknowledge" all active and historical alerts. FUNC-230: Cognitive Digital Twin (CDT) Feedback ● FUNC-231: The CDT module must provide a form interface for users to define "What-if" scenario parameters (e.g., "If Machine X is offline..."). ● FUNC-232: Simulation results must be presented with clear, side-by-side comparisons of "Current State" vs. "Scenario Outcome" using graphs (e.g., bar, line charts).

FUNC-230: Cognitive Digital Twin (CDT) Feedback ● FUNC-231: The CDT module must provide a form interface for users to define "What-if" scenario parameters (e.g., "If Machine X is offline..."). ● FUNC-232: Simulation results must be presented with clear, side-by-side comparisons of "Current State" vs. "Scenario Outcome" using graphs (e.g., bar, line charts). ● FUNC-233: Simulation results must include a "Summary Box" that highlights the key impact (e.g., "Cost: +15%," "Production: -8%").

Y/N," "Is this an anomaly?") must appear in the operator's UI when the system requires human input. The prompt must include an optional free-text field

FUNC-240: Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) ● FUNC-241: A simple validation prompt (e.g., "Confirm this data? Y/N," "Is this an anomaly?") must appear in the operator's UI when the system requires human input. The prompt must include an optional free-text field allowing the user to provide additional comments or explanations. ●

Technical and Architectural Requirements (CODE: TECH) TECH-300: Technology Stack and Responsiveness ● TECH-301: The UI must be developed using HTML5, CSS3, and a modern JavaScript framework (e.g., React, Vue, Angular). ● TECH-302: The interface must be fully responsive, adapting all components

HCD-100: Intuitiveness and Low Learning Curve ● HCD-101: An optional "Onboarding Tour" must be presented to first-time users to introduce main UI components (e.g., Dashboard, Alerts, Simulator). ● HCD-102: Complex workflows (e.g., setting targets in MO-DDSS, creating a CDT scenario) must be designed as a "Step-by-Step Wizard" format, broken into a maximum of 3-4 logical steps. ● HCD-103: A "Progress Bar" must be present in every wizard to show the user their current location in the process.

(e.g., If "Scenario" is used in one module, "Hypothesis" must not be used in another for the same function). ● HCD-106: The platform must provide a centralized Terminology Dictionary that defines and standardizes all key UI and domain terms (e.g., Scenario, Alert, Optimization Target). ● HCD-107: The Terminology Dictionary must be accessible to all partners and UI designers and serve as the authoritative reference for terminology usage across

● HCD-106: The platform must provide a centralized Terminology Dictionary that defines and standardizes all key UI and domain terms (e.g., Scenario, Alert, Optimization Target). ● HCD-107: The Terminology Dictionary must be accessible to all partners and UI designers and serve as the authoritative reference for terminology usage across all modules. ● HCD-108: Any introduction of a new term or modification of an existing term must be reflected in the Terminology Dictionary and reviewed for consistency.

HCD-110: User Diversity and Customization ● HCD-111: The platform must support at least 5 distinct user roles (e.g., Manager, Engineer, Operator, Admin, Developer) with different permission sets.

HCD-110: User Diversity and Customization ● HCD-111: The platform must support at least 5 distinct user roles (e.g., Manager, Engineer, Operator, Admin, Developer) with different permission sets. ● HCD-112: The Admin role shall be responsible for application-level configuration, including default dashboards, role permissions, and KPI definitions. ● HCD-113: The Developer role shall have access to system-wide technical

HCD-110: User Diversity and Customization ● HCD-111: The platform must support at least 5 distinct user roles (e.g., Manager, Engineer, Operator, Admin, Developer) with different permission sets. ● HCD-112: The Admin role shall be responsible for application-level configuration, including default dashboards, role permissions, and KPI definitions. ● HCD-113: The Developer role shall have access to system-wide technical settings, debugging, and integration-related configurations, and shall not be intended for standard operational use.

● HCD-111: The platform must support at least 5 distinct user roles (e.g., Manager, Engineer, Operator, Admin, Developer) with different permission sets. ● HCD-112: The Admin role shall be responsible for application-level configuration, including default dashboards, role permissions, and KPI definitions. ● HCD-113: The Developer role shall have access to system-wide technical settings, debugging, and integration-related configurations, and shall not be intended for standard operational use. ● HCD-114: Admins and Managers must be able to define fixed or predefined

● HCD-112: The Admin role shall be responsible for application-level configuration, including default dashboards, role permissions, and KPI definitions. ● HCD-113: The Developer role shall have access to system-wide technical settings, debugging, and integration-related configurations, and shall not be intended for standard operational use. ● HCD-114: Admins and Managers must be able to define fixed or predefined dashboards that serve as default views for specific user roles. ● HCD-115: Users must be able to hide or show widgets on their main dashboard

● HCD-113: The Developer role shall have access to system-wide technical settings, debugging, and integration-related configurations, and shall not be intended for standard operational use. ● HCD-114: Admins and Managers must be able to define fixed or predefined dashboards that serve as default views for specific user roles. ● HCD-115: Users must be able to hide or show widgets on their main dashboard to personalize their view. ● HCD-116: Dashboard widgets must be configurable with respect to:

● TECH-311: Development must follow "Atomic Design" principles (e.g., Atoms: Button, Molecules: Search Form, Organisms: Header) for reusability. ● TECH-312: All UI components (buttons, forms, color palette, typography) must be defined in a central "Design System" or "Component Library" to ensure platform-wide consistency. ● TECH-313: A new module (e.g., a "Maintenance Module") must be able to be added to the platform navigation and structure with minimal effort and without breaking the existing UI.

TECH-320: Interoperability ● TECH-321: All data exchange with the backend (DKG, CDT, MO-DDSS) must be done via standardized APIs (e.g., RESTful or GraphQL) using JSON. ● TECH-322: A clear "loading" state (e.g., skeleton screens or spinners) must be displayed to the user while data is being fetched (e.g., opening a large report). ● TECH-323: A user-friendly error message (e.g., "Data could not be loaded.
